questions.question: The 4 Ps of marketing are Product, Price, Place, and ___.
options: People
Promotion
Purpose
Platform
questions.explanation: McCarthy's 4 Ps (1960): Product (what you sell), Price (what you charge), Place (where/how you sell), Promotion (how you communicate). Extended to 7 Ps: +People, Process, Physical Evidence.

questions.question: STP in marketing stands for Segmentation, Targeting, and ___.
questions.explanation: STP is the strategic marketing framework: Segment the market into groups, Target the most attractive segments, Position your product distinctly in the minds of the target audience.

questions.question: A brand's positioning should try to appeal to all customer segments simultaneously.
questions.explanation: Effective positioning requires sacrifice — owning a specific idea in the customer's mind. Trying to be everything to everyone results in a diluted, forgettable brand. 'The essence of positioning is sacrifice.' — Jack Trout

questions.question: A digital ad campaign has 1M impressions, 2.5% CTR, 4% conversion rate, and $50 average order value. With a CPC of $0.80, what's the ROAS (Return on Ad Spend)?
questions.formula: ROAS = Revenue / Ad_Spend = (Impressions × CTR × Conv_Rate × AOV) / (Impressions × CTR × CPC)

questions.question: Match each pricing strategy to its description:

right: Low initial price to gain market share quickly
High initial price, gradually reduced as market matures
Free basic version, charge for premium features
Price set by perceived customer value, not cost

questions.explanation: Penetration gains share (Netflix early days). Skimming maximizes early adopter revenue (new iPhones). Freemium converts free users (Spotify). Value-based charges what it's worth to the customer (B2B SaaS).

questions.question: A B2B SaaS company has 10% monthly churn and $500 CAC. Monthly subscription is $49. Is this business sustainable?

scenario.context: Cloud project management tool. 2,000 customers. Growing 5% monthly. NPS: 32.

options: Yes — growth rate (5%) offsets churn and LTV exceeds CAC
No — LTV ($490) barely covers CAC ($500); payback period is 10+ months and churn is unsustainable
Depends on whether they can reduce churn below 5% within 6 months
Yes — if they raise prices to $99/month to improve unit economics
questions.explanation: LTV = $49/month × 10 months = $490. LTV:CAC ratio = 0.98:1 (below the 3:1 minimum for healthy SaaS). The company is spending more to acquire a customer than they'll ever earn back. Must fix retention before spending on growth.

questions.question: Arrange the customer journey stages (AARRR pirate metrics) in order:
questions.explanation: AARRR framework (Dave McClure): Acquisition → Activation → Retention → Revenue → Referral. Each stage is a funnel — optimize the leakiest stage first for maximum impact.

questions.question: What is a 'positioning statement'?
options: A company's mission and vision combined
A concise internal statement defining target audience, category, unique benefit, and reason to believe
The tagline used in advertising campaigns
A legal disclaimer about product capabilities
questions.explanation: Format: 'For [target audience], [brand] is the [category] that [unique benefit] because [reason to believe].' It's internal strategy, not an ad copy — it guides all marketing decisions.

questions.question: A company's email campaign has a 45% open rate but only 0.3% click-through rate. Find the gap:
questions.code:
Subject line: '50% OFF — Today Only! 🔥'
Open rate: 45% (industry avg: 21%)
Email content: 2000-word blog post about product features
CTA: Small text link at the bottom: 'Shop now'
Click rate: 0.3% (industry avg: 2.6%)
options: Clickbait subject creates opens but content doesn't match the promise — expectation mismatch
Email is too long — people opened expecting a deal but found a blog post
CTA is buried and unclear — should be prominent button above the fold
All of the above — subject/content mismatch + poor content + weak CTA
questions.explanation: Triple failure: (1) Subject promises 50% off, content doesn't deliver immediately, (2) 2000 words is far too long for a promotional email, (3) CTA buried as text link vs. prominent button. Fix: match promise in subject, short copy, bold CTA above fold.

questions.question: You're launching a new product in a category dominated by one incumbent (70% market share). What's your go-to-market strategy?
questions.scenario: New cloud-based design tool competing with Figma. Your tool has better AI features but less mature collaboration. Budget: $2M for launch year.

options.label: Head-to-head — compete on features, lower price
Niche first — dominate one underserved segment before expanding
Platform play — build ecosystem/marketplace that incumbent can't replicate
Category creation — redefine the market so you're not compared to incumbent

questions.explanation: Geoffrey Moore's 'Crossing the Chasm': new entrants should dominate a niche (beachhead strategy) where the incumbent underserves. Figma themselves did this — started with web designers before expanding to all product design. Your AI features serve a specific segment better.

questions.question: What does SEO stand for?
options: Social Engagement Optimization
Search Engine Optimization
Sales Efficiency Operations
Strategic Equity Outcomes
questions.explanation: SEO (Search Engine Optimization) is the practice of improving your website's visibility in organic (unpaid) search results. It involves content quality, technical optimization, and building authority through backlinks.

questions.question: A brand's social media followers grew 300% in 3 months but revenue dropped 15%. Diagnose the disconnect:

presentation.context: DTC fashion brand. Hired influencer marketing agency. Instagram went from 50K to 200K followers.
findings: New followers demographics: 60% outside target market (wrong country/age)
Influencer audience: entertainment/comedy, not fashion buyers
Website traffic from social: unchanged (same as before campaign)

options: Vanity metrics — followers are bots or wrong audience; no purchase intent
Algorithm penalty from sudden follower spike
Brand dilution from being associated with non-fashion influencers
Seasonal decline in fashion retail unrelated to social
questions.explanation: Classic vanity metric trap. The influencer brought their audience (entertainment fans) who have no interest in buying fashion. Followers ≠ customers. The brand optimized for the wrong metric. True KPIs: conversion rate, revenue per follower, customer acquisition.

questions.question: Should brands use dark patterns (manipulative UI/UX) to increase conversion rates?
questions.scenario: Examples: pre-checked 'subscribe' boxes, confusing unsubscribe flows, fake urgency timers ('Only 2 left!'), hidden fees revealed at checkout, shame-based opt-outs ('No, I don't want to save money').

options.label: Whatever converts — business exists to maximize revenue
Never — dark patterns erode trust and create long-term brand damage
options.framework: Brand equity — trust is the asset

options.label: Some urgency/scarcity is fine if truthful; manipulation is not
options.framework: Truthfulness — line between persuasion and deception

options.label: Regulate with fines — let the market decide ethics within legal bounds
options.framework: Legal compliance — law as moral minimum

questions.explanation: Genuine scarcity ('3 tickets left at this price') and social proof ('1,247 people bought today') are ethical persuasion. Fake timers, hidden costs, and shame-based language are deception. The FTC increasingly treats dark patterns as unfair trade practices.
